items.q: Where does transcription happen in a eukaryotic cell, and what is the product?
items.correct: In the nucleus — DNA is copied into mRNA, which then travels to the cytoplasm for translation.
items.misconception: Transcription happens at ribosomes, where DNA is copied directly into protein.
items.random_wrong: Transcription happens in the mitochondria during respiration.
items.why: Students conflate transcription (DNA→mRNA, in the nucleus) with translation (mRNA→protein, at ribosomes); they think it's all one process at the ribosome.

items.q: After pre-mRNA splicing in eukaryotes, which sequences remain in the mature mRNA?
items.misconception: Introns remain because they encode the protein; exons are removed as junk.
items.random_wrong: Both introns and exons are removed and the cell uses just the cap and tail.
items.why: Students confuse the EX- (expressed) and IN- prefixes; the naming is the opposite of what intuition suggests.

items.q: Where does transcription occur in a eukaryotic cell?
items.correct: In the nucleus — DNA is transcribed to mRNA, which then travels to the cytoplasm.
items.misconception: Transcription happens on ribosomes in the cytoplasm.
items.random_wrong: Transcription happens in mitochondria where ATP is produced.
items.why: Students mix transcription and translation — translation happens on ribosomes, transcription happens in the nucleus.

items.q: Where does splicing of introns occur?
items.why: Correct answer: Nucleus. “Cytoplasm” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A pre-mRNA has 9 introns and 10 exons. How many phosphodiester-bonded junctions must the spliceosome create to join all the exons together?
items.why: Exact answer: 9. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
